hub_subtitle: Business English para Missões Internacionais e Pequenos Negócios

lesson.menu_title: It Depends On It -- Handling Unexpected Questions and Buying Time
lesson.menu_desc: Handle an unexpected question in a Q&A: buy time, ask for clarification, and answer with care -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 14 -- It Depends On It
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Flávia Florêncio Ferreira | Lesson 14 -- It Depends On It
lesson.grammar_point: conversation management strategies (buying time, redirecting, clarifying)

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: an explanation that makes something easier to understand
to say the same thing again in different words
the situation and facts around something
something you think is true without proof
when people understand something in the wrong way
to guide the talk back to your main point
a short stop before you answer

paras: Flavia is at an international event in Italy. After her talk, it is time for questions. A man asks an unexpected question about prices in Brazil. Flavia does not know the answer at once, so she buys a moment. 'That is a great question. Let me think for a moment,' she says. A short pause is not a problem, because it gives her time to think.

Then Flavia is not sure about the question. She asks for clarification: 'Could you say that again?' The man repeats his question in a different way. Now she understands the context, and she sees a small misunderstanding. The man had a wrong assumption about her project.

Flavia answers with care. 'It depends on the situation,' she says, because the answer is not always the same. Then she adds a nuance: 'What I mean is, small businesses need different support in different markets.' She redirects the talk back to her main message. Her calm answer is clear, and the audience understands the point.
reading.source: Alumni by Better — A2 reading about handling an unexpected question in a Q&A, buying time and asking for clarification

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Flavia leaves the room because she cannot answer any question.
Flavia stays calm, buys time, asks for clarification, and answers an unexpected question with care.
The text explains how to book a flight to Italy.

items: Flavia buys a moment before she answers the unexpected question.
The text says: 'That is a great question. Let me think for a moment.'
Flavia understands the question perfectly the first time.
The text says: 'Could you say that again?' She asks for clarification.
The man had a wrong assumption about Flavia's project.
The text says: 'The man had a wrong assumption about her project.'
Flavia says the answer is always the same in every situation.
The text says: 'It depends on the situation,' because the answer is not always the same.

gapfill.text: Three moves for a hard question: 1) buy time (That is a great question. Let me think for a moment). 2) ask for clarification (Could you say that again?). 3) redirect and clarify (What I mean is...). A short pause is normal and professional.

bank.label: Useful sentences for a Q&A
items: That is a great question. Let me think for a moment.
What I mean is, small businesses need support.
That is a good point. Let me come back to it.
In other words, the context is different in each market.

lf.title: Complete the strategy phrase
items: I am not sure I understand. Could you
That is a great question. Let me

lf.text: Buy time, ask for clarification, then clarify your own point. These moves keep the conversation calm and clear.

items.situation: Someone asks an unexpected question and you need a moment. What do you say?
tips: That is a great question. Let me think for a moment.
That is a good point. Give me a second, please.

items.situation: You did not hear the question well. Ask the person to repeat it.
tips: Sorry, could you repeat the question?

items.situation: The answer is not always the same. Show that it changes.
tips: It depends on the market and the context.

items.situation: You want to explain your point in a clearer way.
tips: What I mean is, small businesses need support.
In other words, the context is different in each market.

items: A visitor asks an unexpected question. Buy time first, then ask them to repeat it. Use 'Let me think for a moment' and 'Could you say that again?'.
Answer a hard question. Say 'It depends on the situation', then clarify your point with 'What I mean is...'.
Handle a full unexpected question: buy time, ask for clarification, answer with care, and clarify your point.
Someone makes a wrong assumption about your project. Politely redirect the talk and explain what you really mean.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model answer
list: Buy time: 'That is a great question. Let me think for a moment.'
Clarify the question: 'Could you say that again?'
Answer with care: 'It depends on the situation.'
Clarify your point: 'What I mean is, small businesses need support.'
answerkey.note: A short pause is normal. Buying time and asking for clarification make you sound calm and professional.

listenings.text: Good afternoon. At the event in Italy, a man asked me an unexpected question about prices. I did not know the answer at once, so I said, that is a great question, let me think for a moment. A short pause is not a problem. Then I was not sure about his question, so I asked, could you say that again? He repeated it, and I understood the context. I saw a small misunderstanding, because he had a wrong assumption. Finally, I answered, it depends on the situation. What I mean is, small businesses need different support in different markets. Thank you.

Good afternoon. Here is one simple tip for hard questions. First, do not panic. Buy time and say, that is a great question, let me think for a moment. A short pause is normal, and it shows that you are calm. Second, if you are not sure, ask for clarification. Say, could you say that again? Third, answer with care. You can say, it depends on the situation. And if there is a misunderstanding, clarify your point and say, what I mean is. These simple moves keep the conversation clear. Good luck!

extra_audio.text: First, someone asks Flavia an unexpected question at the event. Then, she buys a moment and says, that is a great question, let me think. Next, she is not sure, so she asks, could you say that again? After that, she answers and says, it depends on the situation. Finally, she clarifies her point and says, what I mean is, small businesses need support.
